attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with vaginal bleeding and mild lower abdominal cramping for the past six hours. She is eight weeks pregnant according to her last menstrual period. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy multivitamins. She does not smoke.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 118/75 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. Her abdomen is soft and non-tender. Speculum examination reveals a small amount of dark blood in the vaginal vault with a closed cervical os. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a single viable intrauterine pregnancy of 8 weeks and 2 days with positive fetal cardiac activity. No subchorionic haematoma is seen.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Serial serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a threatened miscarriage, characterised by vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy with a closed cervical os and ultrasound confirmation of fetal viability. The presence of fetal cardiac activity at eight weeks gestation is associated with a good prognosis, with approximately 90% of such pregnancies continuing to viability.

Reassurance and conservative management is the most appropriate approach. The patient should be advised that while bleeding may continue, the presence of fetal cardiac activity is reassuring. She should return if bleeding becomes heavy or pain increases significantly.

Serial serum beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements are not indicated when ultrasound has confirmed fetal viability and appropriate growth for dates. These measurements are more useful in cases where pregnancy location or viability is uncertain.

Progesterone supplementation has not been shown to improve outcomes in threatened miscarriage when fetal cardiac activity is present, although it may be considered in cases with a history of recurrent pregnancy loss.

Counselling that miscarriage is likely would be inappropriate and potentially harmful given that the presence of fetal cardiac activity at this gestation is associated with a good prognosis of around 90% for ongoing pregnancy.

Admission for observation is not necessary given the stable vital signs, minimal symptoms, and confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy. The patient can be safely managed as an outpatient with appropriate follow-up.

The key to management is recognising that while vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy is concerning for patients, the presence of fetal cardiac activity is highly reassuring. Regular antenatal care should continue as scheduled.
learningPoints: Threatened miscarriage with a viable fetus on ultrasound requires reassurance and conservative management.
The presence of fetal cardiac activity on ultrasound in first trimester bleeding is associated with a 90% chance of ongoing pregnancy.
Serial beta-human chorionic gonadotropin measurements are not required when ultrasound confirms fetal viability.
references: Jaques AM, Pattison NS, Douglas KA. Early pregnancy bleeding. Aust Fam Physician [Internet]. 2016 May [cited 2024 Nov 27];45(5):283-6. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2016/may/early-pregnancy-bleeding
